design_philosophy.core_loop: Cooperative puzzle-solving and enemy defeat through environmental manipulation: collect items, solve room-based puzzles, use found objects (barrels, bombs, blocks) to eliminate enemies, progress to boss, repeat across themed island areas
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Puzzle comprehension vs. execution under enemy pressure; resource scarcity (single item hold limit); cooperative coordination in multiplayer
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Play as Goofy or Max rescuing friends from pirates by exploring a themed island, using improvised weapons and tools found in the environment
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately designed for two-player cooperation—single-player mode explicitly tedious by design. Refuses direct combat; forces creative use of stage layout and found objects. Early Shinji Mikami work emphasizing puzzle-first, action-second design. Character asymmetry (Goofy slower but stronger, Max faster but weaker) creates complementary roles

difficulty.lives_system: Players start with lives; collecting fruit grants protection (one-hit shields) and extra lives via collection streaks. Red diamonds grant extra lives directly
difficulty.continues_system: Blue diamonds grant continues. In multiplayer, if one player dies, they respawn with three lives when moving to a new screen. Both players dying on same screen triggers continue or password restart
difficulty.checkpointing: Password system allows resume from stage start. No mid-stage checkpoints; bosses appear only at stage end
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Japanese version includes selectable difficulty affecting puzzle complexity. Single-player mode is mechanically harder due to design for two players; multiplayer trivializes content. Players can farm fruit for extra lives

progression.upgrade_system: No permanent upgrades. Temporary item collection (hook, bell, candles, shovels, boards, keys) serves as single-screen puzzle keys rather than character progression
progression.unlock_mechanism: Progressing through five themed stage areas (beach, village, haunted castle, cavern, pirate ship) unlocks subsequent levels. Doors unlock via defeating all enemies on screen or solving block-slide puzzles
replayability_hooks: Multiplayer vs. single-player drastically different difficulty curves
Character selection asymmetry (Goofy vs. Max) encourages replays with different playstyles
Collectible fruit encourages speedrunning/optimal paths for high scores
Password system enables stage select for mastery

world_building_techniques: Theme-based level design (beach → village → castle → cavern → pirate ship creates spatial narrative of island exploration)
Character-driven mission (rescue Pete and PJ creates emotional stake)
Plot twist: mistaken identity of Keelhaul Pete creates narrative payoff
Each level themed around location (haunted castle has appropriate enemies/aesthetics)

boss_design.philosophy: Boss fights are puzzle encounters rather than pattern-memorization duels. Players must use throwable objects spawning in the arena; bosses are defeated via environmental damage rather than direct combat mechanics. Fits overall 'creative problem-solving' design ethos
notable_bosses: Keelhaul Pete (final boss, pirate captain, appears twice)

faction_indicators: Island inhabitants (neutral, can be tools or obstacles)
danger_signals: Pirate ship appearance and abduction initiates plot
Keelhaul Pete's existence and return (whale spitting him out) signals supernatural threat level
environmental_storytelling: Beach setting: fishing trip interrupted by pirate intrusion
Village under siege: populated area corrupted by pirate presence
Haunted castle: suggests island's supernatural properties
Pirate ship finale: literal location of captors, climactic arena
